ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for pork ribs and peanut soup; closest match is pork soup food product (FOODON_03301710). Peanut component not captured in this code.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pork meat, cooked — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for pork-based soup dish; peanut and broth components not individually coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated composite code for pork ribs and peanut soup. A00GJ (cooked pork) used as primary anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one bowl (approximately 350 g) of pork ribs and peanut soup, including broth, pork rib pieces, and softened peanuts.

nutrient_source.title: Pork Ribs and Peanut Soup — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Pork Ribs and Peanut Soup (cr_id F06100032). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for pork ribs, boiled (NDB 10124), proportionally adjusted to match HPB fat content.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate pork-based soup with legume component
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pork ribs and peanut soup in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 14 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) peanuts have a published GI of 14 (University of Sydney GI Database, boiled peanuts); (2) pork ribs contribute negligible carbohydrate; (3) the dish carbohydrate content is 1.05 g/100g, almost entirely from peanuts. Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (14 × 1.05 × 3.5) / 100 ≈ 0.5, classified as very low. Confidence in GI estimate is moderate given the low carbohydrate content of the dish.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 272 mg/100g; typical 350 g bowl delivers approximately 952 mg sodium, representing 41% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a bowl, avoid drinking the full broth, and request reduced salt during preparation to keep sodium intake within safe daily limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (1.05 g/100g) and low GI (estimated 14); peanuts slow gastric emptying and support postprandial glucose stability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This soup is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate load and high protein content; monitor total caloric intake given the moderate fat content from peanuts and pork.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork ribs (estimated 1.8 g/100g) and elevated sodium (272 mg/100g) are the primary cardiovascular concerns; peanuts contribute beneficial monounsaturated fats.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this soup in moderation, limit broth intake to reduce sodium load, and prefer lean pork rib cuts to minimise saturated fat contribution.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork ribs contain arachidonic acid (omega-6), a precursor to pro-inflammatory eicosanoids; peanuts provide some MUFA and vitamin E but do not sufficiently counterbalance the pro-inflammatory effect.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit frequency of consumption of pork rib dishes and consider substituting with fish or chicken to reduce arachidonic acid intake per EULAR dietary guidance.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Ribs and Peanut Soup (排骨花生汤, Páigǔ Huāshēng Tāng) is a traditional Singaporean Chinese slow-cooked soup made by simmering pork ribs with raw peanuts in water for one to two hours until the peanuts are soft and the broth is rich and milky. The dish is a staple comfort food in Singaporean Chinese households and is commonly served as part of a shared meal with steamed rice. The broth is savoury, mildly sweet from the peanuts, and has a light, clean flavour. The pork ribs become tender and fall off the bone, while the peanuts absorb the pork flavour and develop a creamy, soft texture. Nutritionally, per 100 g the soup provides 87 kcal, 9.16 g protein, 5.01 g fat, and only 1.05 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish. Sodium is 272 mg/100g, which is a moderate concern for hypertension at typical serving sizes. The glycemic index is estimated at 14 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management. The dish is not suitable for halal diets due to pork content. It contains peanuts as a declared allergen. Clinically, it is appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low carbohydrate and low GI profile, but sodium intake should be monitored for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The peanut component contributes beneficial monounsaturated fats, while the pork rib component contributes saturated fat and arachidonic acid relevant to inflammatory conditions.
common_user_queries: Is pork ribs and peanut soup healthy?
How many calories in pork rib peanut soup?
Is pork rib soup good for diabetics?
Can I eat pork ribs and peanut soup if I have high blood pressure?
Is pork rib soup high in sodium?
Is pork rib peanut soup gluten free?
What are the nutrients in pork rib soup?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06100032) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy pork ribs boiled entry as HPB does not report this field. GI is an analogous estimate based on published peanut GI from the University of Sydney GI Database, justified by the negligible carbohydrate contribution from pork.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite pork ribs and peanut soup dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (F06100032) and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for this exact composite preparation as of 2026. Saturated fat (1.8 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for pork ribs, boiled (NDB 10124), proportionally scaled to match the HPB-reported total fat of 5.01 g/100g. GI value of 14 is an analogous estimate based on published GI for boiled peanuts (University of Sydney GI Database); the dish carbohydrate is 1.05 g/100g, almost entirely from peanuts, making this estimate defensible. Input dietary_tags 'plant_based' and 'keto' have been removed as they are inconsistent with a pork-containing dish; 'low_calorie' retained as a contextual note only. Occasion tag 'snack' from input retained in meal_context as 'comfort food' but not classified as a primary snack dish.
